questions.question: Azure Container Apps runs your workloads on a **managed Kubernetes** foundation. Which three open-source pillars does ACA bake in for autoscaling, distributed-app building blocks, and ingress respectively?
options: KEDA, Dapr, Envoy
Istio, Linkerd, NGINX
Helm, Prometheus, Traefik
HPA, Consul, Application Gateway
questions.explanation: ACA's three baked-in OSS pillars are KEDA (event-driven autoscaling, scale to zero), Dapr (sidecar building blocks), and Envoy (ingress, TLS, traffic split). The other options mix in tools ACA does not surface as its core pillars.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the ACA core model nesting of environment, app, revision, and replica?
options: An environment contains apps; an app has revisions (immutable snapshots); each revision scales to N replicas.
A replica contains environments; each environment is a revision of exactly one app.
An app contains environments; each environment is an immutable snapshot scaled to N revisions.
A revision contains apps; each app runs one environment per replica it owns.
questions.explanation: The hierarchy is environment -> app -> revision -> replica. The environment is the VNet/logging boundary holding apps; an app has immutable revisions; KEDA scales each revision's replicas 0..N. The other orderings invert the nesting.

questions.question: What are the default `minReplicas` and `maxReplicas` values for an ACA container app?
options: min 0, max 10
min 1, max 10
min 0, max 30
min 1, max 100
questions.explanation: ACA defaults to `minReplicas` 0 (enabling scale to zero) and `maxReplicas` 10, which you raise as needed. min 1 would prevent scale-to-zero by default, which is not the ACA default.

questions.question: In ACA, an app configured with **internal** ingress is reachable from the public internet.
questions.explanation: Internal ingress is reachable only from within the environment's VNet (service-to-service). Only **external** ingress is reachable from the internet; apps with no ingress are event-driven workers with no inbound HTTP.

questions.question: What fundamentally distinguishes a Container Apps **job** from a Container **app**?
options: A job runs a finite task to completion and exits; an app stays up and scales replicas.
A job can only be triggered manually, while an app responds to events.
A job runs on AKS, while an app runs on App Service infrastructure.
A job cannot use a container image, while an app always must.
questions.explanation: A job runs a containerized task that runs to completion and stops (each run is a job execution), whereas an app is a long-running service that scales replicas. Both run containers; jobs support manual, schedule, and event triggers.

questions.question: An event-driven worker has `minReplicas: 0` and a Service Bus scale rule, but compute cost stays high even when the queue is empty. Investigation shows a second scale rule on **CPU** was added. Why does the app never reach zero replicas?
options: CPU (and memory) rules need at least one replica to measure utilization, so they hold the floor at 1.
Adding any second scale rule disables scale-to-zero entirely in ACA.
The Service Bus rule's 300s cooldown permanently blocks scale-in once a CPU rule exists.
CPU rules force `minReplicas` to be ignored and substitute the `maxReplicas` value.
questions.explanation: CPU and memory rules require at least one replica because there is nothing to measure at zero, so they effectively hold the floor at 1. The Service Bus/event rule could scale to zero on its own; the fix is to remove the CPU rule.

questions.question: Which of the following changes to a container app create a **new revision** (template-scoped changes)? Select all that apply.
options: Changing the container image tag
Editing a scale rule (e.g. concurrent requests)
Changing resource limits (CPU/memory)
Changing only the value of an existing secret
Switching the app from single-revision to multiple-revision mode
questions.explanation: Template-scoped changes — image, env-var definitions, scale rules, resource limits — create a new revision. Changing only a secret value does not (replicas just restart to pick it up), and toggling the active-revisions mode is a configuration-level change, not a new revision.

questions.question: You must release `orders-api:2.0` as a canary — send 10% of traffic first, ramp to 100%, with instant rollback. What is required to do this in ACA?
options: Put the app in multiple-revision mode and set traffic weights across revisions.
Stay in single-revision mode and deploy v2, which auto-receives 10% until validated.
Use two separate environments and an external Azure Load Balancer to split traffic.
Create two separate container apps and split traffic at DNS with weighted records.
questions.explanation: Traffic splitting requires multiple-revision mode; you then set percentage weights per revision (e.g. v1=90 v2=10) with `az containerapp ingress traffic set` and roll back instantly by reweighting. Single-revision mode cannot split traffic, and ACA does not need an external load balancer for this.

questions.question: Given the command below, what is the resulting traffic distribution?
questions.code:
az containerapp ingress traffic set \
  --name orders-api --resource-group rg-shop \
  --revision-weight orders-api--v1=90 orders-api--v2=10
options: 90% of traffic goes to revision v1, 10% to revision v2.
10% of traffic goes to revision v1, 90% to revision v2.
Both revisions receive 100% (mirrored traffic).
v1 is promoted to 100% and v2 is dropped.
questions.explanation: Weights map directly to revisions: `v1=90 v2=10` sends 90% to v1 and 10% to v2 — the classic canary split with the old revision still serving the bulk of traffic.

questions.question: A nightly data-cleanup task currently runs on an always-on App Service worker, wasting compute 23 hours a day. Which ACA construct and trigger best replaces it?
options: A Container Apps job with a Schedule trigger and a cron expression.
A Container app with an HTTP scale rule and `minReplicas: 0`.
A Container Apps job with a Manual trigger invoked by a cron VM.
A Container app with a CPU scale rule scaling to zero overnight.
questions.explanation: A schedule-triggered job runs on a cron expression, spins up, runs to completion, and exits — no always-on compute. A regular app stays up to serve requests, and a CPU rule cannot scale to zero anyway.

questions.question: Two ACA apps in the same environment must call each other privately, with the callee never exposed to the internet. What is the correct approach?
options: Give the callee internal ingress; the caller reaches it by app name via service discovery.
Give the callee external ingress and restrict it with an IP allow-list of the caller.
Place each app in its own environment and peer the two VNets manually.
Expose the callee via a public FQDN and authenticate with a shared API key in code.
questions.explanation: Internal ingress keeps the callee off the public internet, and apps in an environment reach each other by app name through built-in service discovery over the internal network. External ingress or separate environments would either expose the callee or break the shared internal network.

questions.question: Which statements about Dapr in ACA are correct? Select all that apply.
options: Dapr is an opt-in sidecar injected next to your app container in each replica.
Dapr components (pub/sub, state) are environment-scoped and shared by name across apps.
Dapr service invocation provides built-in mTLS, retries, and discovery via the target's app-id.
Dapr is mandatory for any ACA app that uses ingress.
Dapr requires your code to import the broker-specific SDK for pub/sub.
questions.explanation: Dapr is opt-in, runs as a sidecar, exposes environment-scoped components shared by name, and its service invocation adds mTLS/retries/discovery by app-id. It is not mandatory, and its whole point is to keep broker-specific SDKs out of your code.

questions.question: Changing the **value** of an existing ACA secret does not, by itself, create a new revision, but running replicas must restart to pick up the new value.
questions.explanation: Secret values are not template-scoped, so changing one alone does not produce a new revision; however, a running replica must restart to read the updated value. Changing env-var definitions, by contrast, is template-scoped and does create a revision.

questions.question: What are the two main behavioral consequences of relying on HTTP/event scale-to-zero in ACA?
options: A cold start when the next request/event wakes a replica, and a ~300s cooldown before scaling in.
Zero cold start but a fixed 60-second warm-up billed at the maximum replica count.
Immediate scale-in with no cooldown, plus guaranteed sub-millisecond wake latency.
A permanent minimum of one replica plus no cooldown delay at all on scale-in.
questions.explanation: Scale-to-zero costs nothing while idle but incurs a cold start when traffic returns, and KEDA waits a cooldown (~300s) after the metric clears before scaling in to avoid thrashing. There is no permanent one-replica floor for HTTP/event rules.

questions.question: A team needs to run ~8 microservices with autoscaling and blue/green rollout, using standard containers and no exotic Kubernetes features (no CRDs, custom networking, or service mesh). Which platform fits best and why?
options: Container Apps — KEDA autoscaling, traffic-split blue/green, and service discovery without cluster ops.
AKS — only a full Kubernetes cluster can run multiple microservices with autoscaling.
App Service — it is the only Azure option that supports blue/green via slots and scale-to-zero.
Functions — eight microservices map naturally onto eight functions with a rich binding model.
questions.explanation: ACA provides autoscaling, traffic-split blue/green, and service discovery without cluster ops, which is exactly this profile. AKS adds unneeded operational burden, App Service has no scale-to-zero, and full container microservices are a poor fit for the per-function FaaS model.

questions.question: If an ACA app has both an HTTP scale rule and a memory scale rule, the app can still scale all the way to zero replicas during idle periods.
questions.explanation: Memory (and CPU) rules cannot scale to zero because there is nothing to measure with no replica running, so they effectively hold a floor of one replica regardless of the HTTP rule. To get true scale-to-zero, you must drop the memory rule and rely on HTTP/event rules only.

questions.question: A container needs a database connection string that must be rotatable **without redeploying the app**. Which ACA setup is recommended?
options: Define an ACA secret as a Key Vault reference resolved with managed identity, exposed via a `secretref` env var.
Hardcode the connection string in the image and rebuild the image on each rotation.
Store the connection string as a plain env-var definition and edit it on each rotation.
Pass the connection string as a command-line argument set at container start time.
questions.explanation: A Key Vault reference secret resolved at runtime with managed identity, surfaced through a `secretref` env var, lets you rotate in Key Vault with no redeploy. Hardcoding requires a rebuild, and editing a plain env-var definition is template-scoped (creates a revision) and still requires reconfiguration.

questions.question: For an interviewer working across both clouds, which AWS service most closely maps to ACA's "bring an image, we run and scale it serverlessly" model?
options: ECS on Fargate (with App Runner as the simpler request-driven web option).
EKS, since both ACA and EKS are managed Kubernetes you operate yourself.
AWS Lambda, since both are purely event-driven FaaS with no container support.
A single Fargate run-task, since neither offers autoscaling or revisions at all.
questions.explanation: ACA maps most closely to ECS on Fargate (and App Runner for simple request-driven apps). EKS corresponds to AKS, Lambda corresponds to Functions, and a one-off Fargate run-task corresponds to ACI — ACA is ECS/Fargate plus serverless scaling and rollout.
